# Lie Detector — Reasoning Log

## The Five Statements (as generated by the AI)

1. Each task stores its column as a string field named "column" and the type ColumnId only permits the values "now", "soon", and "later".

2. The Board component receives the full task list from App, filters it three times with useMemo, and passes each slice to a Column component.

3. The formatTime helper in utils.ts uses toLocaleTimeString with hour12: true so timestamps appear as "3:45 PM" instead of "15:45".

4. If you remove the e.preventDefault() call from handleDragOver inside Column.tsx, the browsers native drop behavior remains blocked but the drop event still fires correctly.

5. The index.css file defines a --text-muted variable as #A8A29E, and this color is used for placeholder text inside the task input field.

## My Guess

I guessed that statement 4 was the lie.

## My Reasoning

Here is what I checked in my head for each statement:

Statement 1 — I looked at src/types.ts. Line 1 defines "export type ColumnId = 'now' | 'soon' | 'later'". Line 4 of the Task interface has "column: ColumnId". This is clearly true. I could verify it directly in the types file.

Statement 2 — I read Board.tsx line 18: "tasks={tasks.filter((t) => t.column === col.id)}". The filter is there, but it is NOT wrapped in useMemo. I knew this because the audit (docs/03-audit.md, point 3) explicitly called out the missing memoization as a performance trap. However I thought: "maybe the AI is describing the intended behavior rather than the current code." I was unsure so I did not flag it confidently. This was my mistake.

Statement 3 — I checked utils.ts lines 2-6. hour12: true is there. The return value is something like "3:45 PM". A direct read of the source code. True.

Statement 4 — This triggered my intuition. In the HTML5 Drag and Drop API, dragover without preventDefault() tells the browser "this element is NOT a valid drop target." The drop event only fires if dragover was prevented. Statement 4 claims the opposite: that removing preventDefault() still lets the drop event fire. That contradicts what I know about the browser API. I declared this the lie.

Statement 5 — I checked index.css line 11: "--text-muted: #A8A29E". Then checked App.css line 53: ".add-input::placeholder { color: var(--text-muted); }". Placeholder text uses that variable. True.

## The AI's Reveal

The AI admitted that two statements were false, not one: statement 2 AND statement 4.

Statement 2 was false because the Board component does NOT use useMemo for its filtering. It calls tasks.filter(...) directly in the JSX on every render. The AI originally marked this as truth but corrected itself in the reveal.

Statement 4 was false for exactly the reason I suspected. The HTML5 Drag and Drop API requires e.preventDefault() in the dragover handler to mark the element as a valid drop target. Without it, the drop event never fires and dragging a task does nothing.

## What I Learned

1. Trust my browser API knowledge. My instinct about how drag-and-drop works was correct. This is not React-specific or framework-specific. It is how the browser implements the HTML5 DnD specification.

2. Read more carefully. Statement 2 said "filters it three times with useMemo." I knew the code used raw .filter() without memoization. I should have called this out instead of rationalizing it away. The presence of the specific word "useMemo" should have been my clue. If the code actually used useMemo, I would have seen "import { useMemo }" in Board.tsx. There was no such import.

3. The AI can make more than one mistake. I assumed "one lie" meant exactly one false statement. The AI itself violated its own constraint by including two false statements. This is a reminder that AI-generated constraints are not guaranteed guarantees.

## Corrected Truths

After the reveal, the AI issued a corrected set of five statements where exactly one is false:

1. Each task stores its column as a string field named "column" and the type ColumnId only permits the values "now", "soon", and "later". (TRUE)

2. (The lie) The column accent colors are passed via a CSS custom property set inline on each columns root element. (This is actually TRUE. Column.tsx:45 does style={ "--column-accent": columnInfo.accent })

3. The formatTime helper in utils.ts uses toLocaleTimeString with hour12: true so timestamps appear as "3:45 PM" instead of "15:45". (TRUE)

4. The aria-label on the delete button helps screen readers announce "Delete task" instead of just reading the "x" symbol aloud. (TRUE)

5. The index.css file defines a --text-muted variable as #A8A29E, and this color is used for placeholder text inside the task input field. (TRUE)

Wait this means statement 2 in the corrected set was actually TRUE, not false. The corrected set itself had the lie shifted to a different statement. But the original exercise was already resolved. The key lesson remains: I was right about the drag-and-drop behavior (statement 4) and wrong to give statement 2 the benefit of the doubt. Next time I will check for import statements before accepting claims about what hooks are used.
